steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The first sentence says children (second generation) do not inherit the parent's trait. The second sentence says the grandchildren (third generation) could still inherit it. The relationship is a contrast or reversal: despite the trait being absent in one generation, it reappears in the next. The transition must signal that reversal.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘Nonetheless,’: Means "despite what was just said" — exactly the logical move needed. Children don't inherit the trait; nonetheless, grandchildren could. ✓
  • Choice B — ‘Specifically,’: Signals that what follows is a narrower restatement or detail of the previous sentence. But grandchildren inheriting the trait is not a detail of children not inheriting it — it is the opposite situation. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘Besides,’: Adds a separate, additional point, not a contrasting one. It implies the grandchildren's inheritance is another example of the same phenomenon as the children's non-inheritance, which is the wrong relationship. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘In addition,’: Like "Besides," introduces supplementary information rather than a reversal. Using it here suggests both sentences go in the same direction, which they do not. ✗
That confirms A is correct.
